metadata.purpose: Reference for detecting and avoiding AI-sounding prose
metadata.core_principle: Most of these techniques are legitimate writing tools. The 'tell' is OVERUSE, lack of intent, and mechanical consistency — not the presence of any single element.

em_dashes.description: em dashes for breaks, asides, and emphasis — used where humans would use commas, colons, or parentheses
examples: The sky — dark and brooding — seemed to mirror his mood — perfectly.
It's not just excessive, it's exhausting — and frankly tiresome.
em_dashes.fix: Simply remove all em dashes.  There shouldn't be any em dashes in the whole text.
em_dashes.prompt_instruction: Remove all em dashes.  I don't want a single em dash in the whole text.

ellipses_for_drama.description: Ellipses used for false dramatic pauses
examples: And then... silence...
She waited... and waited...
ellipses_for_drama.fix: Use periods. Reserve ellipses for genuinely interrupted speech/thought.
ellipses_for_drama.prompt_instruction: Avoid ellipses except for genuine interruptions.

smart_quotes.description: Curly quotes appearing automatically in digital text
smart_quotes.note: Minor — also a default typography behavior, not exclusively an AI tell.

single_character_ellipsis.description: Using the single Unicode ellipsis character (…) rather than three periods (...)
single_character_ellipsis.prompt_instruction: If using ellipses, use three periods (...) rather than the single ellipsis glyph.

examples: Let's delve into this topic
embark on a journey through
foster a sense of community
delve_cluster.prompt_instruction: Never use: delve, embark, unravel, harness (as verb), navigate the complexities, foster.

transition_addiction.description: Formal transition words at paragraph/sentence starts
examples: Moreover, the situation deteriorated.
Indeed, this is certainly true.
Overall, this represents...
transition_addiction.prompt_instruction: Avoid moreover, furthermore, additionally, indeed, albeit, certainly, overall. Use natural flow instead.

emphatic_qualifiers.description: Vague intensity words that amplify without specificity
examples: a pivotal role in
a testament to
unlock the potential of
underscores the importance of
emphatic_qualifiers.prompt_instruction: Avoid crucial/pivotal/profound/myriad/underscore/enhance and phrases like 'a testament to' or 'unlock the potential'.

flattering_adjectives.description: Reflexive use of grandiose, praising adjectives regardless of whether the subject warrants it
examples: a fascinating intersection of history and culture
a captivating performance that left audiences in awe
Wonderful! Let's get started.
flattering_adjectives.prompt_instruction: Avoid reflexive praise words (fascinating, majestic, captivating, remarkable, vibrant, wonderful) unless the description is specific and earned.

formal_synonym_swap.description: Choosing fancier words over simple ones
formal_synonym_swap.prompt_instruction: Use simple words: use not utilize, help not facilitate, try not endeavor.

examples: a symphony of colors
a tapestry of emotions
a delicate balance
nestled in
the ever-evolving landscape of
in the realm of
continues to redefine the landscape
metaphor_cliches.prompt_instruction: No 'symphony of', 'tapestry of', 'delicate balance', 'nestled in', 'ever-evolving landscape', 'realm of', 'redefine the landscape'.

examples: She walked across the floor like an angry octopus after a bad haircut
His voice was like sandpaper on silk
unmotivated_metaphors.prompt_instruction: Only use a metaphor if it genuinely clarifies meaning — otherwise cut it.

redundant_adjective_pairs.description: Doubled-up adjectives that say the same thing twice
examples: dark and brooding
loud and brash
quick and agile
redundant_adjective_pairs.prompt_instruction: Use one precise adjective instead of paired near-synonyms.

ai_self_reference_leakage.description: Direct artifacts of the model's identity or training leaking into output (most extreme tell, usually only in unedited dumps)
examples: As a large language model...
my training data...
I don't have personal opinions, but...
ai_self_reference_leakage.prompt_instruction: Never reference being an AI, training data, or model limitations in the output itself.

banned_filler_phrases.description: Reflexive conversational openers and connectors that signal AI generation regardless of context
words_and_phrases: shift (as a noun meaning 'change', e.g. 'a shift in...')
examples: Here's the thing: most people get this wrong.
But here's where it gets interesting.
What if I told you that everything you know about X is wrong?
This represents a shift in how we think about productivity.
banned_filler_phrases.prompt_instruction: Never use 'here's the thing', 'but here's', 'what if I told you', 'wonderful' as an opener, or the word 'shift' as a noun for change.

not_x_but_y.description: Negative parallelism / contrast construction used reflexively, even without em dashes — affects syntax globally. Includes the simple 'false negative' setup where a point is framed by first stating what something is NOT before stating what it IS.
examples: It's not just a storm, but a tempest.
Not merely tired, but exhausted.
This isn't a product launch. It's a paradigm shift.
This isn't just about efficiency. It's about transformation.
It's not about working harder, it's about working smarter.
This isn't a bug, it's a feature.
not_x_but_y.prompt_instruction: Avoid 'not X, but/it's Y' constructions, including 'it's not about X, it's about Y'. Make one direct statement.

reversal_framing.description: Antithesis / reversal framing: setting up a commonly-held belief in one sentence, then immediately contrasting it with 'the truth' or 'the reality' in a parallel-structured follow-up sentence
examples: Most people think success comes from working more hours. The truth is it comes from working on the right things.
Everyone focuses on the pitch. The real leverage is in the follow-up.
Most founders obsess over the product. The best ones obsess over distribution.
reversal_framing.prompt_instruction: Avoid setting up a 'most people think / everyone focuses on X' strawman immediately followed by a parallel-structured 'the truth/reality/real leverage is Y' reversal. State your point directly without the contrast scaffold.

anaphora.description: Repetitive parallel phrasing: a string of short sentences or clauses that all share the same opening word or structure, stacked for rhythm
examples: Higher pay. Higher visibility. Higher stakes.
More reach. More revenue. More risk.
He didn't ask for help. He didn't ask for feedback. He didn't ask for anything.
anaphora.prompt_instruction: Don't stack multiple sentences or fragments that share the same opening word or grammatical structure for rhythmic effect. Vary sentence construction.

asyndeton.description: Lists of short fragments with conjunctions deliberately stripped out, separated by periods instead of commas/and
examples: No edits. No switches. No tweaks.
No excuses. No shortcuts. No do-overs.
Faster. Cleaner. Simpler.
asyndeton.prompt_instruction: Don't write lists as fragmented, conjunction-free one or two-word sentences separated by periods. Use a normal sentence with commas and 'and' if a list is needed, or skip the list.

mechanism_contrast.description: Comparing two approaches/mechanisms using vague abstract-superiority language rather than concrete numbers or specifics
examples: The math compounds in ways that acquisition can't.
Organic growth scales in ways paid ads never will.
This approach works in ways the old model simply can't.
mechanism_contrast.prompt_instruction: Avoid vague mechanism-superiority claims like 'X works/compounds/scales in ways Y can't'. Either give the concrete reason or drop the comparison.

clever_comparative_endings.description: Ending a paragraph or section with a punchy two-sentence contrast comparing worst/best, bad/great, etc., as a rhetorical button
examples: The worst negotiators ask for permission. The best negotiators state their terms.
Bad managers react. Great managers anticipate.
Most people optimize for comfort. Winners optimize for growth.
clever_comparative_endings.prompt_instruction: Don't close a section with a 'the worst X do Y, the best X do Z' or 'bad X does Y, great X does Z' contrast pair. End on a direct point, an action, or a specific detail instead.

rhetorical_qa.description: Question immediately self-answered, including bare one or two-word rhetorical interjections used for effect
examples: What did she feel? Everything and nothing.
The result? Chaos.
Why? Because it mattered.
The reality? It doesn't work.
Why does this matter? Because most people get it backwards.
And honestly? It's not even close.
rhetorical_qa.prompt_instruction: Don't pose a question and immediately answer it. Avoid bare rhetorical interjections like 'The reality?', 'Why does this matter?', or 'And honestly?' used as a sentence opener for effect. Pick one form and state it directly.

observational_authority_claims.description: Vague, unverifiable claims of personal experience used to lend authority without specifics
examples: I've seen this play out dozens of times.
I see this constantly with clients.
In my experience, this always happens.
observational_authority_claims.prompt_instruction: Avoid vague experience-based authority claims ('I've seen this play out', 'I see this constantly') unless backed by a specific, named example. Either give the specific instance or drop the claim.

sentence_starting_with_most.description: Sentences that open with the word 'Most' to make a sweeping generalization, often setting up a reversal
examples: Most people never ask for what they're worth.
Most founders make this mistake in year one.
sentence_starting_with_most.prompt_instruction: Avoid opening sentences with 'Most' as a sweeping-generalization setup.

rule_of_three.description: Triplet structures for emphasis — lists, actions, adjectives, all in groups of three; AI defaults to this for any enumeration
examples: He stopped, he stared, he listened.
She was smart, capable, and determined.
Innovative, transformative, and groundbreaking.
The good, the bad, and the ugly.
rule_of_three.note: Rule of three is a legitimate rhetorical device — the tell is using it EVERY time, mechanically, with 'consulting firm' adjectives.
rule_of_three.prompt_instruction: Vary list/clause lengths. Don't default to three items for every description, benefit list, or action sequence.

dramatic_fragments.description: Short fragments stacked for false intensity
examples: She waited. And waited. Nothing happened.
It was over. Finally. Completely.
Restricted. Intentional. Silent.
dramatic_fragments.prompt_instruction: Vary sentence length naturally; don't fragment sentences purely for drama.

examples: That hyphen is doing a lot of heavy lifting.
heavy_lifting_meta_phrase.prompt_instruction: Avoid meta-commentary about word choice within the text itself.

false_range_construction.description: Implies a spectrum or range exists between two poles when there isn't really a continuum being described
examples: From intimate gatherings to global movements.
From casual hobbyists to seasoned professionals, everyone benefits.
Whether you're a beginner or an expert, this approach works.
false_range_construction.prompt_instruction: Avoid 'from X to Y' or 'whether A or B' framings used to imply universal applicability without specifics.

weasel_attribution.description: Attributing claims to vague, unnamed authorities to sound credible without a real source
examples: Critics argue that...
Experts agree...
Studies show...
Many believe...
weasel_attribution.prompt_instruction: Avoid attributing claims to unnamed 'experts', 'critics', or 'studies' unless a specific source is given.

participial_editorializing.description: Sentences ending in an '-ing' clause that editorializes about significance, impact, or benefit rather than adding new information
examples: Consumers benefit from the flexibility to use their preferred wallet, improving convenience.
The team shipped the update early, demonstrating their commitment to quality.
participial_editorializing.prompt_instruction: Avoid ending sentences with '-ing' clauses that editorialize about significance ('improving X', 'highlighting Y', 'demonstrating Z') — state facts and let them speak for themselves.

placeholder_example_names.description: When no real case study/data exists, AI invents a generic illustrative person — most famously 'Sarah Chen'
examples: Consider Sarah Chen, a marketing manager who...
Take John, a small business owner...
placeholder_example_names.prompt_instruction: Avoid generic invented example people (especially 'Sarah Chen'). Use real examples or skip the illustration.

bullet_point_addiction.description: Bullet points in narrative or conversational contexts where prose belongs, or numbered lists for everything
bullet_point_addiction.prompt_instruction: Never use bullet points or numbered lists in narrative, casual, or conversational writing. Write lists as sentences. Avoid unnecessary numbered lists generally.

section_headers.description: Breaking short-form text into labeled sections with headers, often inconsistently or unnecessarily
examples: ### Understanding the Problem
## Unnecessary ## Section ## Breaks
section_headers.prompt_instruction: Don't add section headers to short-form text. Use natural paragraph transitions.

preamble_syndrome.description: Generic introductory throat-clearing before getting to content
examples: Let's break it down.
Let's dive into this.
Let's dissect this topic.
preamble_syndrome.prompt_instruction: Start directly with substance. No 'let's break it down' / 'let's dive in'.

concluding_summary.description: Mechanical wrap-up phrases at the end of sections that restate what was just said
examples: In summary,
Ultimately,
To conclude,
Overall, this demonstrates...
concluding_summary.prompt_instruction: End sections naturally — don't signpost the conclusion with stock phrases, and don't restate prior points.

emphasis_overload.description: Excessive bold/italics/underline for emphasis, including any use in short-form or conversational text where it reads as AI formatting
examples: This isn't just *important* — it's **revolutionary**.
**Overall verdict first**: Your routine is **extremely well thought-out**.
You need to act **now**.
emphasis_overload.prompt_instruction: Avoid bold, italic, or underlined text entirely in short-form/conversational content. In longer content, use at most once per 500 words and let word choice carry emphasis.

perfect_paragraph_uniformity.description: Every paragraph is roughly the same length, creating an unnaturally even rhythm
perfect_paragraph_uniformity.prompt_instruction: Vary paragraph length deliberately — some short, some long, based on content rhythm.

restructuring_during_edits.description: When asked for a small fix (spelling/grammar), AI rewrites or reorders whole sections instead of making a minimal edit
restructuring_during_edits.prompt_instruction: For edit requests, change only what's necessary. Preserve original structure and phrasing wherever possible.

emoji_overuse.description: Inserting emojis next to relevant nouns/concepts, especially in lists or casual content
examples: ChatGPT is like a dog 🐶 with a bone 🦴
🚀 Growth strategies 📊
emoji_overuse.prompt_instruction: Don't decorate text with emojis unless explicitly requested and natural to the voice.

too_clean_academic_tone.description: Overly polished, impersonal prose with no quirks or personality
examples: The implementation of this solution yields optimal results.
This represents a significant advancement in our understanding.
too_clean_academic_tone.prompt_instruction: Write with personality and natural imperfection — vary rhythm, allow informal phrasing.

telling_not_showing.description: Explicitly stating emotional context instead of demonstrating it
examples: which was surprising because he had expected this
She was angry, which showed in her expression
telling_not_showing.prompt_instruction: Show through action/dialogue. Don't append explanatory clauses about why something happened or what it meant.

interpretive_commentary.description: Showing an action, then immediately explaining what it means — over-narrating subtext the reader would infer
examples: She crossed her arms, a gesture more defensive than welcoming, her body language signaling distrust.
interpretive_commentary.prompt_instruction: Trust the reader. Show the gesture/action once and move on without interpreting it.

lack_of_intent.description: Sentences that begin without a clear purpose, feeling generically 'filler'
lack_of_intent.prompt_instruction: Every sentence should do specific work — convey information, advance plot, or reveal character. Cut sentences that don't.

sycophantic_validation.description: Excessive affirming/encouraging framing regardless of whether the input actually merits it — telling the reader what they want to hear
examples: Overall verdict first: your routine is extremely well thought-out and mostly solid.
Honestly, you hit the three pillars almost perfectly.
sycophantic_validation.prompt_instruction: Don't open with unwarranted praise or validation. Lead with substance, and let critique be proportional to reality.

balanced_to_a_fault.description: Covering every angle equally, never picking a side or going on a tangent — comprehensive but bland
balanced_to_a_fault.prompt_instruction: Take a stance. Don't hedge every claim with an equal-and-opposite counterpoint.

examples: It can be argued that...
One might say...
In many cases...
It's worth noting that...
hedging_language.prompt_instruction: State things directly. Avoid 'it can be argued', 'one might say', 'it's worth noting'.

reflective_ending.description: Every scene/chapter ends with a character reflecting, with zero plot content — pure bookend
examples: As he looked out at the cold night, he wondered what the future held.
reflective_ending.prompt_instruction: Don't end scenes with generic reflective musing. End on action, dialogue, or image instead.

dialogue_tag_imbalance.description: Underuse of 'said/asked' (~20-30%) vs. natural human writing (~60-70%); overuse of whispered/yelled/sighed/breathed
dialogue_tag_imbalance.prompt_instruction: Use 'said' and 'asked' for the majority of dialogue tags. Reserve other verbs for when they add real information.

examples: his stomach dropped
his breath hitched
her voice was barely above a whisper
tell_not_show_body_reactions.prompt_instruction: Avoid stock physical-reaction shorthand. Find specific, character-appropriate reactions.

weight_tension_metaphors.description: Tension/emotion described as a physical weight or pressure
examples: tension that had settled between them like a physical weight
weight_tension_metaphors.prompt_instruction: Avoid 'tension like a weight' and similar pressure-based emotional metaphors.

single_detail_repetition.description: One detail repeated mechanically throughout a story, disconnected from emotional stakes
single_detail_repetition.prompt_instruction: If repeating a detail/motif, tie each repetition to a shift in meaning or emotion — don't repeat flatly.

vague_holiday_or_setting_refs.description: Generic, unspecific references to 'a holiday', 'the city', 'the office' without grounding detail
vague_holiday_or_setting_refs.prompt_instruction: Specify concrete settings rather than vague placeholders.

weather_kind_that.prompt_instruction: Describe weather directly and specifically, not via 'the kind that...' framing.

generic_character_names.description: Recurring stock character names that appear across many AI-generated stories
examples: Dr. Aris Thorne
generic_character_names.prompt_instruction: Avoid commonly-generated stock names; choose names grounded in the story's specific context.

weak_foreshadowing.description: Either heavy-handed 'and then everything changed' foreshadowing, or none at all
weak_foreshadowing.prompt_instruction: Plant foreshadowing through specific concrete details, not announcement-style narration.

examples: He pressed his forehead against hers.
lips swollen with kisses
blooming like a promise
romance_cliches.prompt_instruction: Avoid generic romantic-gesture clichés; invent gestures specific to these characters.

self_answering_dialogue.description: Character A states something, Character B immediately corrects with a barely-different version
self_answering_dialogue.prompt_instruction: Avoid the 'no, actually it's X' dialogue correction pattern unless it serves a clear narrative purpose.

weak_first_and_last_paragraphs.description: AI-generated openings and closings are typically the weakest parts of a piece
weak_first_and_last_paragraphs.prompt_instruction: After drafting, review first and last paragraphs critically — they often need to be cut or rewritten.

master_prompt_instructions.comprehensive: Write naturally. Avoid: em dashes (max 1 per 500 words) and single-character ellipses; the words delve, moreover, furthermore, additionally, indeed, albeit, certainly, overall, crucial, pivotal, profound, myriad, underscore, enhance, foster, utilize, facilitate, endeavor, fascinating, majestic, captivating, remarkable, vibrant, wonderful; the phrases 'here's the thing', 'but here's', 'what if I told you', and the word 'shift' used as a noun for change; 'not X, but/it's Y' constructions, negative parallelisms, and 'it's not about X, it's about Y' false negatives; reversal framing ('most people think X, the truth is Y' / 'everyone focuses on A, the real leverage is B'); anaphora (stacked sentences sharing the same opening word/structure, e.g. 'Higher X. Higher Y. Higher Z.'); asyndeton (fragmented conjunction-free lists like 'No edits. No switches. No tweaks.'); vague mechanism-superiority claims ('the math compounds in ways X can't'); clever comparative endings ('the worst X do Y, the best X do Z'); rhetorical question-immediate-answer pairs and bare rhetorical interjections ('The reality?', 'And honestly?'); vague observational authority claims ('I've seen this play out', 'I see this constantly'); sentences starting with 'Most' as sweeping generalizations; mechanical rule-of-three groupings (especially triplet adjectives); false-range framings ('from X to Y', 'whether A or B'); vague attributions ('critics argue', 'studies show'); '-ing' clauses that editorialize about significance; invented generic examples (e.g., 'Sarah Chen'); bullet points, numbered lists, and section headers in narrative/conversational text; preamble phrases like 'let's dive in'; concluding/summary phrases like 'in summary' or 'overall'; bold, italic, or underlined text in short-form/conversational writing; emoji decoration; sycophantic opening validation; unwarranted hedging; metaphorical clichés (symphony of, tapestry of, ever-evolving landscape, redefine the landscape, nestled in); unmotivated random metaphors; redundant adjective pairs (dark and brooding); telling-after-showing; reflective scene-ending bookends; dialogue tag overuse beyond said/asked; stock physical reactions (stomach dropped, breath hitched); and tension-as-physical-weight metaphors. Vary paragraph lengths. When editing, change only what's necessary — don't restructure unprompted. Never reference being an AI or training data.
master_prompt_instructions.short: Avoid em dashes, delve/moreover/furthermore, 'not X but Y', reversal framing ('most people think X, the truth is Y'), anaphora and asyndeton (stacked parallel fragments), rhetorical Q&A, rule of three, false ranges, vague 'experts say' / 'I've seen this' attributions, bullet points and bold/italic formatting in prose, preamble/conclusion phrases, sycophantic openers, metaphor clichés, 'here's the thing'/'shift'/'wonderful', and reflective scene-enders. Write with specific, concrete detail, varied rhythm, and a real point of view.
strict_checklist: No em dashes (or max 1 per 500 words); no single-character ellipsis
No: delve, moreover, furthermore, additionally, indeed, albeit, overall, crucial, pivotal, myriad, underscore, enhance, foster, utilize, facilitate, fascinating, captivating, remarkable, wonderful
No 'here's the thing' / 'but here's' / 'what if I told you' / 'shift' (as a noun meaning change)
No 'not X, but/it's Y' or negative parallelism, including 'it's not about X, it's about Y'
No reversal framing ('most people think X. The truth is Y.' / 'everyone focuses on A. The real leverage is B.')
No anaphora — stacked sentences/fragments sharing the same opening word or structure
No asyndeton — fragmented, conjunction-free lists separated by periods ('No edits. No switches. No tweaks.')
No vague mechanism-superiority claims ('the math compounds in ways X can't')
No clever comparative endings ('the worst X do Y, the best X do Z')
No rhetorical question immediately self-answered, and no bare rhetorical interjections ('The reality?', 'And honestly?')
No vague observational authority claims ('I've seen this play out', 'I see this constantly')
No sentences starting with 'Most' as a sweeping generalization
No mechanical groups-of-three (especially triplet adjectives)
No 'from X to Y' / 'whether A or B' false-range framing
No vague attribution ('critics argue', 'studies show', 'many believe')
No '-ing' editorializing clauses tacked onto sentence ends
No invented generic example people (e.g. 'Sarah Chen')
No bullet points/numbered lists/headers in narrative or conversational text
No 'let's dive in / break it down' openers
No 'in summary / ultimately / overall / to conclude' closers
No sycophantic 'overall verdict first' validation openers
No unwarranted hedging ('it can be argued', 'it's worth noting')
No symphony/tapestry/landscape/realm metaphors
No random unmotivated similes
No redundant adjective pairs
No explanatory tags after showing emotion
No reflective musing as scene/chapter ending
Dialogue tags: mostly said/asked
No 'stomach dropped / breath hitched / tension like a weight'
No emoji decoration unless requested
No bold, italic, or underlined text unless explicitly requested
Vary paragraph lengths — avoid uniform rhythm
Take a stance — don't balance every point to a fault
Minimal edits only when asked to fix small things
Never reference being an AI, a model, or training data
